items.title: Global liquidity $193.5T but underlying conditions remain fragile — risk appetite peaked
items.tweetA: Michael Howell's global liquidity hit $193.5 trillion last week. And he's calling it: risk appetite has peaked. PBoC tightening. Fed support fading. Dollar strong. The number is big. The direction is down. Watch EM.
items.tweetB: $193.5T in global liquidity and conditions are still 'fragile.' PBoC tightening. Fed pulling back. Flows reversing from EM and China to US. The liquidity clock is turning — Howell has been right before.

items.title: BoJ hiking to 1% — first time since 1995; synchronized global tightening accelerating
items.tweetA: BoJ hiking to 1%. First time since 1995. ECB hikes Thursday. CPI tomorrow could force the Fed's hand. The synchronized global tightening trade is not over — it's accelerating. Markets are still priced for cuts.
items.tweetB: 1995. That's how long it's been since Japan had 1% rates. Think about the yen carry trade built on zero for 30 years. If 0.25% in August 2024 caused a flash crash, what does 1% do over time to assets priced in cheap yen?

items.title: CPI consensus 4.2% y/y = 3-year high; HY OAS at 275bps never confirmed the equity selloff
items.tweetA: CPI consensus tomorrow: 4.2% y/y, a 3-year high. And yet HY OAS never widened past 275bps during this equity selloff. Credit isn't calling this a recession. It's calling it a buying dip. One of them is wrong.
items.tweetB: The entire equity scenario matrix is one number away from flipping. Phil Dauber: core 0.4%+ = bear flag accelerates. Core 0.3% = bounce rents another week. Core 0.2% = washout complete, S&P targets 7,600 into FOMC. Wednesday is the fulcrum.

items.title: Iran announces end of military ops; but Polymarket peace deal probability collapses to 15% (from 80% two weeks ago)
items.tweetA: Iran says military ops are over. Trump says deal is in 'final throes.' Polymarket says 15% odds of a permanent deal by June 30 — down from 80% two weeks ago. The market has seen this movie before. Skepticism is rational.
items.tweetB: Two weeks ago, Polymarket priced an Iran peace deal at nearly 80%. Today: 15%. Hard-liners pushing Trump to 'finish the job.' Israel hitting Hezbollah. Both sides think they're winning. This doesn't end on a handshake by June 30.

items.title: Hormuz functionally closed — tanker traffic 90% below normal; oil paradoxically falling while HFIR goes long
items.tweetA: Hormuz is 90% closed to tanker traffic. Polymarket puts Iran deal odds at 15%. And oil is falling. HFIR just bought $285K in BNO Jan 2027 calls at $60 strike. The market is pricing in a deal with an 85% chance of not happening by June 30. $BNO $CL
items.tweetB: Oil is falling while Hormuz tanker traffic is 90% below normal. Either the market sees a deal coming that Polymarket doesn't — or there's a mispricing building in energy that won't last. HFIR chose a side with $285K in long-dated calls.

items.title: Pentagon blacklists Alibaba, Baidu, BYD as PLA-linked; DoD procurement ban effective June 2027
items.tweetA: Pentagon listed Alibaba, Baidu, and BYD as companies supporting China's military. Not sanctions — yet. But from June 2027, no US DoD procurement, direct or indirect. The US is formally treating Chinese civilian and military industry as the same thing.
items.tweetB: The Pentagon's China PLA list isn't just about defense procurement. It's the US government treating Alibaba, Baidu, and BYD as strategic threats. Every corporate procurement officer reading this is updating their China vendor policy today.

items.title: UN issues strongest-ever Cuba sanctions criticism: 'children are dying because of the US embargo'
items.tweetA: UN Commissioner: 'Children are dying in Cuba because doctors lack medical supplies.' The embargo has intensified since 2020. Frankopan: the calculus that Cuba's regime will break before its people do is 'mistaken.' Authoritarian regimes don't negotiate that way.
items.tweetB: The Cuba embargo has been running since 1962. The regime is still there. The UN is now calling it a humanitarian crisis. At what point does a 64-year policy get re-evaluated on results rather than ideology?

items.source: Bloomberg / Simon White (via Dark Side of the Boom)
items.title: Equity supply trap opens: the negative net supply tailwind is turning into a headwind
items.tweetA: Simon White's equity supply trap: the buyback-driven negative net supply tailwind is reversing. AI firms, private giants IPOing, listed companies all issuing shares simultaneously. Households at max equity allocation. The market's structural buyer is becoming a structural headwind.
items.tweetB: Everybody's focused on earnings and the Fed. Simon White is watching equity issuance. When gross issuance peaks, markets have historically been near a top. That wave is building right now. SpaceX IPOs Friday. The tap is open.

items.title: Gold at $4,321 refuses to rally on Iran de-escalation — rates/dollar repricing is the dominant force
items.tweetA: Gold at $4,321, near March lows, refusing to rally on Iran de-escalation. Dauber's read: rates and dollar repricing are the dominant force, not geopolitics. Hot CPI tomorrow could be brutal for gold. The safe-haven bid is broken.
items.tweetB: Hormuz 90% closed. Iran risk elevated. Gold near its March lows. Something is wrong with this picture. The metal is pricing a CPI spike tomorrow, or real rates are doing something the gold market sees that equities don't.

items.title: SPY bear flag: 3rd consecutive close below 21 EMA; BofA 70% of bear-market signals triggered
items.tweetA: SPY: 3rd straight close below the 21 EMA, bear flag forming, 8/21 EMA cross approaching. BofA: 70% of bear-market signals triggered. Next support: 720 (50-day), then 700 (21-week). Only bullish if CPI tomorrow is clean. Everything is riding on one print.
items.tweetB: Bear flag. Sub-21 EMA. Third day. VIX still under 20. BofA's bear signal checklist is 70% checked. The market needs clean CPI tomorrow or the technical breakdown accelerates to 720 then 700 on SPY.

items.title: ECB hiking Thursday to 2.25%; China exports +15% / imports +26% YoY — domestic demand not collapsing
items.tweetA: ECB hiking Thursday to 2.25%. BoJ at 1%. And China's imports are expected +26% YoY. The synchronized global tightening is real — but China showing +26% import demand contradicts the deflationary doom loop narrative. These two can't both be fully right.
items.tweetB: ECB +25bps Thursday = 2.25% deposit rate. Every major central bank except the Fed is tightening. Meanwhile China imports +26% YoY says domestic demand is not collapsing. The market is holding two narratives simultaneously that don't easily coexist.

items.title: AI engineering jobs +60% YoY vs SWE +7%; Anthropic is the #1 most-desired employer at 35%+ of coaching requests
items.tweetA: Anthropic is now the single most-desired employer for software engineers — 35%+ of all Interviewing.io coaching requests, more than Google (17%) and OpenAI (16%) combined. AI engineering jobs are up 60% YoY. The war for AI talent has a clear frontrunner.
items.tweetB: AI engineering job openings: +60% in one year. Regular SWE openings: +7%. This isn't a gradual shift — it's a reallocation. And the engineers chasing those AI jobs? They want Anthropic first, Google second, OpenAI third. That order matters.

items.title: Anthropic: $965B valuation (>OpenAI), IPO filed, Karpathy hired, 80% two-year retention — industry best
items.tweetA: Anthropic: $965B valuation. Filed for IPO. Recruited Andrej Karpathy. 80% two-year retention — best in the industry. The US government called them a 'supply chain risk' for refusing autonomous weapons contracts. Engineers saw that as a reason TO join.
items.tweetB: The US government called Anthropic a supply chain risk and gave OpenAI the Department of War AI contract. Two months later, Anthropic has the most in-demand job brand in tech, a $965B valuation, and Andrej Karpathy. The intended signal was read exactly backwards.

items.title: Frontend engineers disappearing; interns halved; new grad hiring 1-in-10 (was 3-in-10 in 2023)
items.tweetA: Frontend engineers are disappearing. Intern intake at large tech is half of what it was. Only 1 in 10 engineering hires in 2025 were new grads — down from 1 in 3 in 2023. AI is restructuring the entire shape of the engineering workforce in real time.
items.tweetB: Large tech companies are now taking half as many interns as before. That's a 2-4 year lag before you feel the trained-talent shortage. The funnel is being squeezed at the bottom while AI demand is exploding at the top. This resolves badly for someone.

items.title: Oracle backlog $553B; SpaceX renting 220K+ GPUs to Anthropic; Google paying ~$920M/month for compute
items.tweetA: Oracle backlog: $553B. SpaceX renting 220K+ GPUs to Anthropic. Google paying $920M/month for compute. These are current run rates, not projections. The AI capex cycle is not slowing. The question is whether revenue catches up.
items.tweetB: SpaceX is now in the GPU rental business — renting 220,000+ chips to Anthropic. Oracle has $553B in cloud backlog. The hyperscaler infrastructure war has a new participant: Elon's rocket company is becoming a data center company.

items.title: Apple drops Gemini-powered Siri at WWDC — AI to 2 billion devices; stock -5%
items.tweetA: Apple unveiled Gemini-powered Siri at WWDC. 2 billion devices. Dauber calls it 'the biggest AI monetization partnership of this cycle.' Stock fell 5%. The market is reading margin compression. I'd read it as the distribution moat just got wider.
items.tweetB: Google's AI is now on 2 billion Apple devices. Every other AI company's distribution strategy just got harder. The Apple-Google AI partnership is the new default — you have to beat the default to matter.

items.title: Bipartisan House bill: $300M AI safety center + federal preemption of all state AI laws
items.tweetA: A bipartisan House bill would put $300M into AI safety evaluation AND federally preempt all state AI laws. One national standard replacing California, Colorado, Texas, and every other state's framework. The corporate AI lobby has wanted this for years. It's moving.
items.tweetB: The $300M AI safety center is the headline. The real story is federal preemption of state AI regulation. If this passes, 50 different state AI laws become one federal standard. Most consequential deregulatory move for AI since Section 230.

items.title: 99.86% of journal entries now automated; B2B companies have 4x more period-end work than B2C
items.tweetA: 99.86% of journal entries automated. B2B companies have 4x more period-end accounting work than B2C. The monthly close is dying — 98% of companies using AI-native ERP have already eliminated it. NetSuite and Dynamics have a real problem.
items.tweetB: The month-end close ritual is a relic. Rillet data: 99.86% of journal entries fully automated, 98% of companies no longer do a traditional close. And B2B enterprises have 4x more work to automate. The back office disruption is underway.

items.title: NYC is 4-6 months behind SF on AI adoption; Claude Code just arriving in New York
items.tweetA: NY Tech Week takeaway: NYC is '4-6 months behind SF on AI adoption.' Non-tech workers just now adopting Claude Code while SF moved to Codex. There's a wave of AI adoption building in the world's largest financial and media market that hasn't hit yet.
items.tweetB: 50,000 people at NY Tech Week. NYC's non-tech knowledge workers are 'just now adopting Claude Code.' SF already moved to Codex. The adoption curve starts in SF, hits NYC 4 months later, hits everywhere else 6-12 months after that. That's a predictable, tradeable pattern.

items.title: 7 of top 10 LLMs on OpenRouter are Chinese; US gov in talks for 1-5% OpenAI equity stake
items.tweetA: 7 of the top 10 most-used LLMs on OpenRouter are from Chinese companies. The US government is reportedly in talks to take 1-5% equity in OpenAI. Two data points: the market is choosing Chinese models on performance, the government is choosing OpenAI for control.
items.tweetB: Chinese LLMs: 7 of top 10 on OpenRouter. US government response: take equity in OpenAI and appoint them the Department of War AI supplier. The market is voting one way with API calls, the government another way with national security policy. This tension doesn't resolve quietly.

items.title: ZCash Orchard bug found by Claude Opus 4.8 — AI is now an active participant in protocol security research
items.tweetA: Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.8 found a 4-year-old zero-day in Zcash's shielded pool that could have enabled undetectable counterfeit supply. AI is now doing protocol-level security research. Every legacy smart contract exists in a shrinking window before AI finds what humans missed.
items.tweetB: Claude found a bug in ZCash that humans missed for 4 years. The same capability is available to attackers. The window for undiscovered vulnerabilities in legacy contracts is closing fast — by AI on both sides of the table simultaneously.

items.title: OpenServ: 107x performance per dollar vs standard inference; 70x+ cost reduction vs GPT-5
items.tweetA: OpenServ claims 107x performance per dollar vs standard inference and 70x+ cost reduction vs GPT-5 using structured reasoning graphs where large model plans once, cheap model executes repeatedly. If benchmarks hold in production, enterprise AI economics just changed.
items.tweetB: Separate planning from execution: large model writes the Mermaid diagram plan once, small model runs it on repeat. OpenServ: 107x performance per dollar, 99% reasoning accuracy, auditable trail for regulated industries. Worth watching. arXiv 2512.15959.

items.title: SpaceX IPO Friday: $1.8T valuation, $75B raise, $150B orders (2x oversubscribed), 30% retail
items.tweetA: SpaceX IPO Friday. $1.8T valuation. $75B raise. $150B in orders — 2x oversubscribed. 30% retail allocation. Multiple indexes changed their construction rules to include it. This is simultaneously the largest IPO in history and a live test of Simon White's equity supply trap thesis.
items.tweetB: $1.8 trillion makes SpaceX worth more than Amazon, approaching Apple. It IPOs Friday. The 30% retail allocation means price discovery happens live. Every index fund that changed its rules becomes a forced buyer. Simon White's gross issuance alarm is ringing. $SPCX

items.source: Tiger Research / Henry Kim & Ryan Yoon
items.title: Binance, Bybit, Coinbase, Kraken all launching stock trading — crypto volume down 80%; 'this downturn will be harder than prior ones'
items.tweetA: Binance launched stocks. Bybit added TradFi perps. Coinbase went commission-free on stocks. Kraken bought a futures broker for $1.5B. Crypto spot volume is down 80% from peak. Tiger Research: this bear market will be harder than prior ones because exchanges are now growing without crypto.
items.tweetB: Hyperliquid: 23 of 30 top perp assets are now stocks and commodities, not crypto. This isn't a product tweak — it's a category migration. Crypto exchanges are becoming stock exchanges. The thing they were supposed to disrupt is what's keeping them alive.

items.title: ZEC fell 40% (~$3B wiped) on Orchard bug; Railgun (RAIL) caught bid to 2026 highs on 10x volume
items.tweetA: ZEC fell 40% ($3B wiped) on the Orchard bug despite NO confirmed supply breach. RAIL (Railgun) immediately caught bid to 2026 highs on 10x volume. Market rotating from consensus-layer privacy (ZEC) to application-layer privacy (RAIL). Vitalik-backed, $91.88M TVL ATH.
items.tweetB: The ZCash bug taught the market a lesson: when privacy is baked into the chain's consensus, one circuit flaw = total confidence collapse. Application-layer privacy (Railgun) keeps blast radius contained. $3B of ZEC market cap just found a new address.

items.title: 59% Kalshi odds BTC hits $50K before $100K; Fear & Greed at 9; $302M 24h liquidations
items.tweetA: Kalshi: 59% odds BTC hits $50K before $100K. Fear & Greed at 9. $302M in 24h liquidations. BTC ETFs at second-highest weekly outflow ever. And Strategy is buying BTC at 2x the rate miners produce it. One of these signals is very wrong.
items.tweetB: Fear & Greed at 9 means the crowd is at peak fear. 59% chance BTC sees $50K before $100K per prediction markets. Strategy is buying 2x what miners produce. These signals don't all resolve in the same direction — watch which one is the smart money.

items.title: ETH staking ATH 32.6% of supply staked; Strategy buying BTC at 2x mining rate; BitMine down ~$10B
items.tweetA: ETH staking just hit an ATH: 32.6% of total supply staked. Strategy buying BTC at 2x the mining rate. BitMine holds 4.59% of all ETH and is down $10B. Supply is tightening in both chains simultaneously while price falls. That's a compression spring.
items.tweetB: Strategy absorbs bitcoin at 2x the mining rate. 32.6% of ETH is locked in staking. Supply shrinking in both chains simultaneously. The market is pricing 59% odds of $50K BTC before $100K. At some point these two facts can't both be true.

items.title: GEODNET: DePIN RTK GPS with 21,000+ stations in 145 countries — picks-and-shovels for the robot economy
items.tweetA: GEODNET provides 1-2cm GPS accuracy (vs meters from standard GPS) via 21,000 DePIN stations in 145 countries. Every delivery drone, humanoid robot, and autonomous tractor needs this. 80% of revenue burned. Picks-and-shovels for the robot economy that nobody is talking about.
items.tweetB: 1-2 centimeter GPS accuracy. Required for: delivery drones, humanoid robots, self-driving tractors. Standard GPS gives you meters. GEODNET gives you centimeters via 21,000 physical stations worldwide. Most people have never heard of RTK correction. Every robot will need it.

items.title: Jefferies raises copper peak to $8/lb by 2030-31 — 33% above prior forecast; aluminum also raised
items.tweetA: Jefferies raised its copper peak forecast to $8/lb by 2030-31 — a 33% increase from their prior $6-6.50 target. Also raised aluminum. Preferred plays: $FCX $GLEN $AAL $TECK $AA. Electrification + AI data center power demand thesis getting formal bank coverage.
items.tweetB: $8/lb copper by 2030-31. That's Jefferies' new peak forecast, up 33% from prior. The electrification + data center demand thesis is structural, not cyclical. $FCX is the most direct equity expression. $GLEN adds commodity trading optionality.

items.title: AI-designed universal coronavirus vaccine: first human trial shows antibodies against multiple strains simultaneously
items.tweetA: Cambridge/DIOSynVax just completed the first human trial of an AI-designed universal coronavirus vaccine. Recipients developed antibodies against multiple coronaviruses simultaneously. If it scales: no more variant chasing. AI designed the super-antigen. Journal of Infection, this month.
items.tweetB: An AI designed a vaccine that teaches the immune system to fight the entire coronavirus family — not one variant, the whole genus. First human trial: it works. Frankopan's conclusion: 'when we run into trouble again with emerging infectious disease, it won't be because of a coronavirus.'

items.title: 'Indexing is no longer neutral': S&P changed decade-old rules for SpaceX; quality at lowest multiples in years
items.tweetA: Multiple stock indexes just changed their decade-old rules to include SpaceX at IPO. Rebound Capital: 'indexing is no longer the safe, neutral choice.' When the index changes rules for a company, passive investing has become active management by committee.
items.tweetB: S&P 500: ~13% earnings growth at 27x EV/EBIT = PEG of 2.5x. Rebound Capital portfolio: 19% EBIT growth at the same 27x = PEG of 1.4x. Same valuation. Better growth. Different companies. Quality is being ignored while the market stares at AI.

items.title: IPO lies thesis: every participant lies in the same direction — and nothing changed since Spitzer
items.tweetA: Former Salomon Smith Barney equity derivatives head: every IPO participant lies — issuer, founders, banks, institutions, retail, regulators. Fidelity inflated orders 10x. Founders sold via collars while claiming conviction. Nothing changed since Spitzer. SpaceX IPOs Friday.
items.tweetB: Pure retail in an IPO: 'only lies to themselves.' Full fills on large orders = the deal will trade down. FOMO market orders at open = paying the top tick. The author processed thousands of these at Citi. He's not being cynical: 'it's reality. It works. It's just messy.'

items.title: Clarity Act heading to Senate; MiCA enforcement July 1; UK FCA proposes 10% crypto ETN allocation for funds
items.tweetA: Clarity Act heading to Senate floor. MiCA enforcement July 1. UK FCA proposing 10% crypto ETN allocation for authorized funds. US, EU, and UK all expanding crypto regulatory legitimacy in the same week. This is the institutional infrastructure the next bull cycle runs on.
items.tweetB: July 1: MiCA enforcement begins. That's 3 weeks. Unregulated exchanges serving EU users must comply or lose 450 million people. Clarity Act has 200+ org support. UK may allow 10% crypto in regulated funds. The regulatory backdrop just got materially cleaner.

items.title: DIEM: perpetual compute as yield-bearing transferable asset — the treasury primitive for AI agents
items.tweetA: Venice's DIEM token: lock sVVV, get a perpetual right to $1/day of API compute forever. It's a yield-bearing compute asset — you can lend it, trade it, price a forward curve on inference costs. 'Hedge AI opex like airlines hedge fuel.' Best crypto framing I've seen this cycle.
items.tweetB: What does an autonomous AI agent use as treasury? DIEM: a perpetual claim on $1/day of API inference, forever. No credit card. No human top-up. Venice is building the budget primitive for the agent economy. 3M users, 10x API growth since January.

items.title: xAI secretly trained on Claude outputs for months before getting cut off
items.tweetA: xAI secretly trained on Claude outputs for months before getting cut off. If Grok was trained on Claude data, every benchmark comparison between Grok and Claude is suspect. And Anthropic's training data purity just became a durable competitive moat.
items.tweetB: Model-on-model data theft is the IP story of 2026. xAI was caught training on Claude outputs. What happens when the AI companies with the most resources start systematically scraping the best-performing models? Clean training data becomes the scarce asset.

items.title: ARK Venture Fund invests in Cellares Series D — fully automated cell therapy manufacturing
items.tweetA: ARK Venture just invested in Cellares (Series D) — a fully automated cell therapy manufacturing platform. The CAR-T bottleneck isn't the drug; it's manufacturing: custom, expensive, days per patient. Automate it and the addressable market multiplies. Biotech picks-and-shovels.
items.tweetB: CAR-T therapy: remarkable efficacy, manufacturing nightmare. Each dose is custom-made per patient, taking days. Cellares is automating the entire process. ARK Venture invested at Series D. Retail can access via ARKVX, $500 minimum.
